title: How a Search Engine Works
kicker: a field course · for new engineers
subtitle: A ground-up walkthrough of how a tiny search engine turns a folder of documents into instant, ranked answers. We build the mental model one layer at a time — from a single parsed file, to the index, to a live query.

intro.text: The whole system has two halves. A **build** phase runs offline and is entirely deterministic: it reads a folder of documents and compiles them into one compact index. A **serve** phase runs online: it answers a query by scoring documents against the index. Understanding the build is what makes the serve make sense — so we start there.

intro.title: How to read this course
intro.body: Each section builds on the previous one. Use the cards below to jump around, or the *next / previous* links at the bottom of every page. Toggle light/dark from the top-right.

footer: **How a Search Engine Works** — a self-contained field course. Every page is generated from a single JSON content model; nothing here is hand-written HTML.

sections.title: Why it exists
sections.summary: The problem, the big picture, and the two-phase design.

blocks.text: Finding one paragraph in a folder of ten thousand files is slow if you read every file on every query. A search engine pays that cost **once**, up front, so every query afterwards is instant.

The trick is a data structure called an *inverted index*: instead of mapping documents to the words they contain, it maps each word to the documents that contain it. A query then becomes a set lookup, not a scan.

rows: Reads every document once, extracts its text, and writes a compact index to disk. Deterministic and offline.
Loads the index into memory and answers queries by scoring documents. Fast and online.

blocks.title: Mental model
blocks.body: Think of the build as writing the index at the back of a textbook, and the serve as a reader using that index to jump straight to the right page.

sections.title: Parsing a document
sections.summary: Turning a raw file into a clean stream of tokens.

blocks.text: Before anything can be indexed it must be *tokenized* — split into lowercase words with punctuation stripped. A parser reads one file and emits a list of tokens plus a little metadata.

blocks.title: parse.js — the whole parser
blocks.code:
export function parse(text) {
  return text
    .toLowerCase()
    .replace(/[^a-z0-9\s]/g, ' ')   // strip punctuation
    .split(/\s+/)                    // split on whitespace
    .filter(Boolean);                // drop empties
}

items: The output is a flat list of tokens — nothing more.

blocks.title: Pitfall
blocks.body: Never index the raw file bytes. Binary files, markup, and encoding noise all pollute the index and wreck ranking. Parse first, always.

sections.title: Building the index
sections.summary: From token lists to an inverted index on disk.

blocks.text: The index is a map from each token to the list of documents that contain it (a *postings list*), plus how often it appears. We also record each document's length — ranking needs it.
The index is written once and loaded into memory at startup. Individual documents are only re-read when a result needs to be displayed.

sections.title: Ranking with BM25
sections.summary: Why rare words matter more, and how length is normalised.

blocks.text: Not every matching document is equally relevant. BM25 is the scoring function that decides the order — it rewards rare query terms and dampens the advantage of very long documents.

rows: more occurrences score higher — but with diminishing returns.
a word in *few* documents is more informative than a common one.
a short document matching a term beats a long one that merely mentions it.

blocks.title: the intuition, in one line
blocks.code: score(doc, query) = Σ  idf(term) · saturating_tf(term, doc) · length_penalty(doc)

blocks.title: Why not just count matches?
blocks.body: Raw counts favour long documents and common words. BM25's *saturation* and *length normalisation* are exactly the corrections that make ranked results feel right.

sections.title: A query, end to end
sections.summary: One search traced from keystroke to ranked answer.

blocks.text: Here is the full path a single query takes, using everything from the previous sections.

blocks.caption: The query path: the same tokenizer parses the query and the documents; matches are scored and sorted before the top results are read back.
blocks.code:
flowchart LR
  U([user query]) --> P[tokenize]
  P --> L[[inverted index lookup]]
  L --> S{BM25 score & sort}
  S --> R[read top docs]
  R --> A([ranked answer])
  B[(documents)] -->|same tokenizer| P

items: The user types `how does ranking work` — the only unpredictable input in the system.
The query is **parsed** with the exact same tokenizer used at build time.
Each token is looked up in the **inverted index** to gather candidate documents.
Candidates are **scored** with BM25 and sorted.
The top results are read from disk and returned with a snippet.

blocks.title: The key symmetry
blocks.body: The query and the documents pass through the *same* parser. If tokenization differs between build and serve, matches silently disappear. Keep them identical.

blocks.text: That's the whole engine: parse once, index once, then score on every query. Everything else is optimisation.
